I've got enough context from the spec and rationale to start building a plan. Let me quickly check those couple ADRs I haven't reviewed yet and scan the rest of the documentation, then see what's already in the repo.

The repo is completely empty — just docs and config files, no source code yet. This is a greenfield .NET 10 Blazor Server application with a massive scope: three main projects plus test suites, a complex PostgreSQL schema with twenty-plus tables, custom identity stores with Dapper, Argon2id hashing, WebAuthn passkeys, TOTP, rotating tokens, device pairing, an event-sourced order model, kitchen operations, billing, admin features, observability, containerization, and end-to-end tests. The user is asking me to create a staged implementation plan specifically to avoid hitting session limits, so I need to break this into manageable chunks that can be executed sequentially.

I should align with the spec's own milestone structure but organize each stage to be self-contained and resumable. In a single session, I can realistically set up the solution skeleton, transcribe the full database schema from the spec, build the Domain layer (which is pure and well-specified), scaffold the DataAccess layer, create the WebApplication with health checks and observability, set up containerization, and write some foundational tests. The database schema is already provided verbatim in the spec, which is a significant shortcut.

The user's key point is that I need to document a staged plan clearly so a future session can pick up where I left off. Before diving in, I should verify what tooling is available — specifically whether .NET 10 is accessible in my environment. Given the network restrictions, I'll need to check what's available through the ubuntu archives or other allowed sources rather than relying on direct Microsoft downloads.

I'm also noticing the GitHub PAT the user provided might be useful if there's additional code in the actual repository beyond what's in the dump, though the dump should represent the current committed state. checking the repo via GitHub API to verify whether there's been any code committed since the dump. I'll use the PAT to avoid rate limits, but I'll keep it secure in an environment variable and never print it. Once I confirm the latest state matches the dump, I can proceed with a clean implementation.

Then I need to assess what tools are available locally — whether .NET and git are set up — since that determines if I can actually compile and test as I go, or if I'm just writing code for the user to run later. I have the architectural decisions from the ADRs already, so I can move straight into building the implementation plan.

I'll create a progress tracker file (something like BUILD_PROGRESS.md) to mark what's been done and what's next, since this is a working artifact rather than formal documentation. The actual build happens in my container under /mnt/user-data/, not in the read-only project copy, so I'll organize everything there and make sure the user can pick it up and continue.

I'm thinking through the structure carefully — the repo already has docs, LICENSE, and other files, so I'm only generating the new code and infrastructure files (src/, tests/, compose.yaml, Containerfile, Caddyfile, run.sh, scripts/, and an updated README). I'll mirror the repo layout in my output directory and focus on completing Stage 0 in this session.

For Stage 1, I'm planning to build out the full initial database schema, the domain layer with UUID v7 generation, join token logic, Argon2 password handling, event projection, validation rules, and the obligations state machine, plus the data access layer with DbUp migrations and the web application entry point.

I also need to set up the infrastructure files—Dockerfile, Docker Compose for dev and production, Caddy reverse proxy config, deployment scripts for backups and tunneling, and a README. I'll round it out with some domain unit tests covering the token vectors, password encoding round-trips, and event folding.

That's already substantial, and stages 2 through 6 covering identity stores, passkeys, TOTP, and the Blazor UI would be massive. I should be realistic about scope and aim for a clean stopping point where someone could pick up the work later. Let me check the environment and repo structure first, then reassess how much of the domain layer I can actually write well in this session. No skills apply to writing backend source code in this stage, so I can proceed without reading any documentation. I'm checking what tools are available in this environment—no .NET, Docker, or PostgreSQL—but git and Node are there. I'm trying to install the .NET 10 SDK, but the network only allows specific domains and Microsoft's repositories aren't among them, so that won't work either.

Confirming OS and that the .NET install infrastructure is not reachable under the network allowlist
I'm on Ubuntu 24.04 without .NET installed, and the network allowlist is blocking access to the .NET download infrastructure, so I can't compile or run tests locally. I'll write the code carefully and have the user verify it on their Fedora machine where the toolchain is available.

Now I'm laying out a staged plan aligned with the spec's milestones but broken into resumable chunks. Stage 1 covers the solution scaffolding, project files, and the entire Domain layer—the foundation that everything else depends on. I'll start with the core identifiers, value objects, and domain entities, then move through aggregates and domain events. I'm setting up the infrastructure layer with a clock abstraction for testability, then moving into data access with embedded SQL migrations managed through DbUp, a connection factory using Npgsql, and configuring the web application entry point to bind configuration.

In Program.cs I'm wiring up OpenTelemetry instrumentation for traces, metrics, and logs across ASP.NET and Npgsql, running migrations before the HTTP server starts, exposing health check endpoints, and scaffolding the Blazor Server host with configuration validation that enforces HTTPS origins, Argon2 parameters, and token rotation minimums.

The Blazor components and configuration classes are minimal stubs to get the project building, and I'm defining the containerized infrastructure with Docker and Compose configurations for both development and production environments.

I'm also adding domain unit tests that document the core logic—token vectors, password hashing round-trips, and the obligations pipeline—plus backup/restore scripts and a progress tracker to resume work later.

Next up is Stage 2, where I'll implement the Dapper-based identity stores for users, passwords, lockout policies, two-factor authentication, authenticator keys, recovery codes, and passkeys using Argon2id hashing.

For Stage 2, I'm building out password hashing with Konscious's IPasswordHasher (including semaphore guards and rehashing logic), TOTP services with recovery codes, WebAuthn passkey endpoints for .NET 10, lockout enforcement, and a /setup bootstrap wizard with an advisory lock to prevent race conditions. I'll add roles, policies, security event logging, and admin user management, then test everything against a real Postgres database using Testcontainers.

Stage 3 covers the restaurant_table repository with full CRUD operations, secret rotation, display pairing via device cookies, token generation and validation endpoints with metrics, and the join flow with sittings and membership tracking. I'll write integration and component tests for this layer.

Stage 4 moves into the ordering system with a living order repository and locking protocol to handle concurrent updates.

For Stage 4, I'm implementing the order locking mechanism with proper isolation levels, building out the staging UI, batch sending with domain validation, staff edits, fulfillment and reversal logic, projection reads, and view-fold equivalence tests. I'll also add the kitchen display surface with alerts and a background service for reminders.

Stage 5 handles billing and administration—bills, price adjustments, close and settle operations, end-of-day processing, counter fallback QR codes, and menu management with event tracking and post-close corrections.

Stage 6 focuses on hardening for production with full Playwright E2E tests covering all scenarios, backup and restore drills, cloudflared production configuration, tunnel documentation, and a quick-tunnel demo script with warnings.

Now I'm checking the documentation against the code and verifying the CI pipeline with GitHub Actions. I'll start with Stage 1, building directly in the outputs directory to avoid unnecessary copying, though I'll be careful to keep things organized as I iterate.

I need to think through the domain code carefully now.

I'm setting up the core infrastructure: a UUIDv7 identifier factory that leverages .NET 9's built-in Guid.CreateVersion7() method, and a clock abstraction to handle time operations consistently across the domain.

For the join token validation, I'm implementing an HMAC-SHA256 based algorithm that rotates every 60 seconds by default. The token generation combines the table UUID and a window index derived from the current Unix timestamp, then encodes it with Base64Url (without padding). During validation, I'll check both the current and previous time windows using constant-time comparison to prevent timing attacks, and track whether tokens are expired or invalid based on how far back they fall within a bounded lookback window.

For the implementation details, .NET 9 provides System.Buffers.Text.Base64Url which handles encoding without padding automatically. For the UUID formatting, I'll use ToString("D") which produces the standard lowercase hyphenated format that .NET uses by default.

Now I'm setting up the core service with the necessary imports and defining the validation result enum, then starting to build out the JoinTokenService class.

Writing service methods... Checking older windows within the lookback period to see if the token matches any of those, which would mark it as expired rather than invalid, then computing the window index from the current timestamp and rotation interval, and finally implementing the matching logic that compares the presented token against a computed hash for a given window. Writing HMAC computation... Base64Url.DecodeFromChars takes a character span and throws on invalid input, while EncodeToString returns unpadded base64. I'm structuring the Argon2 PHC string format with a record for parameters and methods to format and parse the complete string including salt and tag.

For base64 encoding without padding, I'll use standard base64 (not URL-safe) by encoding with Convert.ToBase64String then stripping the padding characters, and for decoding I'll re-pad to a multiple of 4 before converting back. Now I'm defining the C# types and methods to handle this.

Defining the Argon2 PHC string format... Parsing the parameters from the hash string... Finishing the base64 decoding logic to handle padding, then moving on to the PairingCode implementation which generates 8-character codes from a 32-character alphabet using a cryptographically secure random number generator.

For the hashing, I'm thinking a small Sha256 utility in the Domain layer makes sense since the spec requires hashing the pairing code, device secret, and recovery codes — it's pure and only depends on the BCL. Since the alphabet has exactly 32 characters (5 bits each), modulo bias isn't an issue when using RandomNumberGenerator.GetBytes because 256 divides evenly by 32.

Actually, .NET 8's RandomNumberGenerator.GetItems<T>() is perfect here — it gives me an unbiased, cryptographically strong way to generate the random pairing code directly.

Defining the pairing code generator...

Now I'm creating helper utilities for SHA256 hashing and other security operations, with methods to hash both raw bytes and UTF-8 strings.

For recovery codes, I'll generate 10 single-use codes at enrollment in a readable format like XXXXX-XXXXX using an unambiguous alphabet, storing them hashed. The domain layer will provide a RecoveryCode.Generate() method while the actual hashing happens in the data access layer.

For device secrets, I'm adding a helper to generate 32 random bytes encoded as Base64Url.

I'm keeping the Domain layer focused on what's explicitly specified: IdentifierFactory, JoinTokenService, Argon2PhcString, PairingCode, RecoveryCode, Sha256Hashing, the order model with its fold and validator, the obligations pipeline, the broadcaster interface, and IClock. Device secret generation will live in DataAccess instead.

Now for the order model and fold—the core piece. Events carry order_event_identifier, guest_order_identifier, sequence_number, event_type, actor_person_identifier, actor_role, and occurred_at, with operations nested inside them. To reconstruct order_current_line, I need to track when a line gets added via line_added operation (with order_line_identifier, menu_item_identifier, quantity, unit_price_amount, customization_note), then apply any subsequent price_adjusted operations to get the current unit price, and determine fulfillment status from the latest fulfillment event for that line.

For order_current_state, I'm aggregating pending and fulfilled line counts, computing the total amount by summing quantity times current price across non-removed lines, and capturing first_submitted_at from the earliest guest submission and last_event_at from the most recent event overall. I'm defining Domain types in C# that mirror the database schema—enums for OrderEventType (covering guest submissions, staff edits, price adjustments, fulfillments, and reversals) and OrderActorRole (guest, kitchen, counter, administrator).

Now I'm modeling operations as a discriminated union using an abstract base class with concrete record subclasses, since C# doesn't have native union types. This gives me a clean fold pattern where each operation type—like LineAddedOperation—inherits from OrderOperation and carries its specific fields such as menu item identifier, quantity, and unit price.

Defining operation records... Defining the event record structure... Defining the event fold logic... Within a single event, a line can't be both fulfilled and reverted since operations must alternate and target distinct lines, though the spec doesn't explicitly forbid multiple price adjustments on the same line—but in practice staff UIs send one operation per event. The view's LATERAL with ORDER BY sequence DESC LIMIT 1 creates nondeterminism when operations share the same sequence number, so my fold needs to apply operations within an event in a deterministic order to match that behavior.

I'll process events by ascending sequence, applying operations within each event in their given list order so the last one wins, and I'll add a comment noting this assumption since the equivalence tests generate sequences respecting the validation rules that avoid same-line ambiguity within a single event anyway.

Now I'm building the OrderMutationValidator to check a proposed batch of operations against the current projection and context—whether the sitting is open, who the owner is, whether they're a member, and if menu items are active. ection belongs to another order or doesn't exist, I reject it. The validator should take a proposed event with the actor's role and operation intents, plus a context object holding all the facts it needs—actor membership, order ownership, sitting state, current projection showing line states and ownership. For guest submissions, I also need the menu item lookup and active flags, plus the server-side unit price so I can validate quantity ranges. I'll structure this as a list of operation errors indexed by position, each noting which line (if any) failed and why. Before I finalize the implementation, I want to make sure the approach stays manageable for Stage 1.

The validator's pure rule logic is genuinely valuable and testable in isolation, so I'll implement that core engine now with a clean input contract, even though the full transaction integration belongs in M4 when the persistence layer is wired in.

Defining the input records for proposed operations... Defining the ProposedOrderEvent record with required properties for event type and actor role, along with collections for various order modifications like line additions, removals, price adjustments, and fulfillment changes.

I'm also setting up an OrderMutationContext record to capture validation context about whether the actor owns the order, is a sitting member, and if the sitting is currently open.

For the validator, I realize the current projection doesn't include removed lines, which I need to validate removal operations properly. I'm planning to enhance the projection with a LineStates dictionary that tracks each line's state including whether it's been removed, so the validator has complete visibility into the line history. Defining the ledger record...

Now I'm working through the validation rules: the event must contain at least one operation, any referenced line identifiers must already exist in the ledger, and for guest submissions there are additional constraints around actor role and context—the actor must be the order owner and a sitting member with an open sitting. When adding lines, I need to verify the menu items exist and are active, with quantities between 1 and 100.

For removals in a guest submission, the line being removed must have been added by a guest submission from the same actor, be in pending status, and not already removed. Guest submissions can only contain adds and removals—no price adjustments, fulfillments, or reversals allowed. A removal also can't target a line that was added in the same event, and fulfillments target pending lines while fulfillment reversals target fulfilled ones.

For price adjustments, they target non-removed lines and require a non-empty reason, with access limited to counters and administrators. After the sitting closes, only administrators can make changes, and only through staff edits, price adjustments, fulfillments, or reversals—guest submissions are blocked entirely.

If any operation fails validation, the entire event is rejected. Staff can add or remove any line regardless of state, but can't remove already-removed lines or add duplicates within the same event. I'm also enforcing type-to-role mappings at the validator level to mirror the schema constraints: guest_submission maps to guest role, staff_edit to kitchen/counter/administrator, price_adjustment to counter/administrator, and fulfillment operations to their respective roles.

Each event type must carry only its corresponding operations—guest_submission and staff_edit allow LineAdds and LineRemovals, while price_adjustment, fulfillment, and fulfillment_reversal each carry only their specific operation types.

Now I'm building out the validator as a pure function that checks this shape thoroughly, then moving on to implement the ObligationsPipeline state machine that sequences post-authentication requirements: password change, then TOTP enrollment, then destination access.

Defining the obligations logic... Defining the broadcaster interface...

I'll put the interface and notification types in the Domain layer now, then implement the in-process broadcaster in WebApp during the first milestone and wire it up through dependency injection. That way it's ready for the UI to consume later. Now moving on to the database schema. Writing DDL schema... Extracting the DDL and views blocks...

Now I need to set up the .NET project structure with .NET 10, central package management through Directory.Packages.props, and a global.json to pin the SDK version. I'll add reasonable package versions for the dependencies, keeping in mind they may need updating. I need to figure out which Identity packages are actually needed for .NET 10 Blazor Server — some types come from the framework reference while others require explicit NuGet packages, so I'll need to be careful about what to include in the dependencies. I'll add the framework reference to the project file, then pull in Argon2 for password hashing in Stage 2. For observability, I'm wiring up OpenTelemetry with instrumentation for ASP.NET Core, the runtime, and Npgsql—capturing traces, metrics, and logs through OTLP. I'll also need FIDO2 for passwordless authentication.

For testing, I'm setting up xunit with NSubstitute for mocking, Testcontainers for PostgreSQL integration tests, Playwright for end-to-end testing, and WebApplicationFactory for API testing. I'll start with a Domain.Tests project in Stage 1 and scaffold the others minimally, adding bunit later for Blazor component tests.

Since I can't verify exact versions here, I'm estimating plausible ones for the .NET 10 era: OpenTelemetry around 1.10+, Dapper 2.1.x, Npgsql 10.0.0 to align with .NET 10, and DbUp 6.x. I'll flag these as estimates and note that dotnet restore will handle version resolution.

For the WebApplication in Stage 1, I need to build Program.cs with config binding and validation that fails fast, OTel configuration, migrations running at startup before the app binds, health check endpoints for liveness and readiness, and a minimal Blazor Server interactive page to serve as a landing point.

I'll skip area routing and auth for now since those are M2+, and just create a single Home component that confirms the app is running. Now I need to work through this systematically—extracting the SQL schema first, then creating files in batches starting with the plan document, solution and project files, and the Domain layer.

Now I'm setting up the solution structure, starting with the root-level configuration files like global.json, Directory.Build.props, and the project file, along with standard dotfiles like .gitignore and .editorconfig.

I'm pinning the .NET 10 SDK and configuring common build properties across all projects—targeting net10.0, enabling nullable reference types and implicit usings, treating warnings as errors, and centralizing package version management. I'm also considering InvariantGlobalization and how it interacts with timezone and currency formatting needs.

On second thought, I'll leave InvariantGlobalization disabled to ensure TimeZoneInfo and culture-specific formatting work properly, and I'll make sure the container includes tzdata. For TreatWarningsAsErrors, I'm torn between enforcing clean code and the risk that a stray warning could block the build since I can't compile-verify—I'll set it to false for safety while still keeping code style enforcement enabled.

For the solution file, I'm considering using the newer .slnx format that .NET 10 supports, which is much simpler to hand-author than the traditional .sln with its GUIDs. I'll use MyRestaurant.slnx and set up the XML structure... I'm torn between using .slnx for its cleaner syntax and modern tooling support versus a classic .sln for maximum compatibility. Since the project targets .NET 10 and the spec doesn't mandate a specific format, .slnx makes sense, but I should probably generate a traditional .sln with proper GUIDs to be safe with any legacy tooling that might not recognize the newer format yet. Writing solution configuration... Writing OrderModel.cs...

Now the order event model — enums, event/operation records, and projected shapes.


Scrutinizing OrderProjection and validator specifications
Scrutinizing OrderProjection and validator specifications

Order event model: enums, events, operations, projected line/state, ledger
I'm working through the OrderProjection.FromEvents method now, which needs to process events sequentially and maintain a working state for each order line. For each line, I'm tracking the order line identifier, menu item, quantity, current unit price (which gets updated by price adjustments), customization notes, when it was added, and who added it.

The algorithm sorts events by sequence number, then iterates through each operation: LineAdded creates a new working entry, LineRemoved marks it as removed, LinePriceAdjusted updates the current unit price to the latest value, and LineFulfilled/LineFulfillmentReverted toggle the fulfilled state. I'm also tracking the first submission time across all GuestSubmission events and the latest event timestamp overall, then building the final ledger ordered by the sequence in which lines were added.

To ensure deterministic ordering, I'll maintain a Dictionary keyed by line identifier plus a separate List tracking the add order, assigning each line an incrementing index as it's created. This way the ledger can be ordered by that index, and the equivalence test can compare by identifier to avoid fragility while still providing a stable output order.

Show less

The pure order projection fold (§8.5) — equivalent to the SQL views

I'm examining the underlying structure of what's already in place.


List all files created so far
Now I'm checking the current state of the codebase and reviewing what still needs to be completed in Stage 1—the OrderMutationValidator design that was outlined in the transcript, plus the DataAccess project infrastructure like the database connection factory and schema migration runner, and the WebApplication setup with configuration and observability.

Before I start writing new code, I need to examine the existing Domain files to ensure consistency with the namespaces, OrderModel structure, and OrderProjection that the validator will depend on.

Crystallizing capability matrix for OrderMutationValidator implementation
Crystallizing capability matrix for OrderMutationValidator implementation

Locate §6.5 in the dump

Read §6.5 validation invariants verbatim
I'm checking the capability matrix to see which roles have permissions for each action.

Done
Now the capability matrix at §6.4 and the ordering-model context around it.


Verifying Domain completion, scrutinizing conventions before DataAccess
Verifying Domain completion, scrutinizing conventions before DataAccess

Read §6.4 capability matrix
I'm mapping out the capability matrix for the OrderMutationValidator, defining which roles can perform specific actions like guest submissions, staff edits, price adjustments, and fulfillment operations, each with their own permission constraints.

Now I'm documenting the invariants that govern how these operations work: every event must contain at least one operation, all referenced order lines must exist in the ledger, removals have specific restrictions based on actor role and line state, guest submissions require the actor to be the order owner and member of an open sitting with valid menu items and quantities, operations within the same event can't reference each other, and fulfillment operations target pending lines while reversals target fulfilled ones.

Post-closure, only administrators can perform staff edits, price adjustments, fulfillment, or reversals—never guest submissions. The validator uses an all-or-nothing approach where any failed operation rejects the entire event and returns per-operation error details along with a fresh projection.

The validator itself is a pure domain function that takes the proposed event with its type, actor role, and operations, plus the current projection of the ledger and lines, along with contextual facts like whether the actor owns the order, is a sitting member, if the sitting is open, and per-item details like existence, active status, server unit price, and quantity constraints. I'm now defining the input record structures for the proposed operations: line additions include the order line identifier, menu item identifier, existence and active flags, server unit price, quantity, and customization notes, while removals include the identifier and reason, and price adjustments include the identifier and new unit price.

The design keeps the function pure by having the caller resolve menu facts upfront and pass them into the proposed line add record.

Now I'm defining the validation result structure with an error code enum and human-readable messages, plus a validation result that collects per-operation errors. Quantity needs to be between 1 and 100 per the spec.

For the invariant about removals not referencing lines added in the same event, I need to check within a single proposed event whether any removal targets a line that was just added.

For guest actors specifically, removal is only valid if the line was added by their own guest submission event and is currently pending—meaning it hasn't been removed or fulfilled yet.

Staff actors have broader permissions and can remove any non-removed line, including fulfilled ones, since the "currently fulfilled" restriction applies only to guests. The spec allows staff to remove fulfilled lines as long as they haven't already been removed.

Now I'm mapping operation types to their allowed event shapes: guest submissions can only contain line additions and removals (no price adjustments or fulfillment changes), staff edits allow additions and removals of any line, while price adjustments, fulfillment, and fulfillment reversals each carry only their specific operation type. I need to enforce that each event type strictly carries only its permitted operations.

Let me verify the existing services and conventions in the codebase—checking JoinTokenService, Argon2PhcString, SecretGenerator, and Clock to ensure I'm following the same namespace patterns, sealed classes, and XML documentation style. The Domain.csproj appears to support C# 12 with collection expressions, so I'm ready to write the OrderMutationValidator.

For the validator design, I'm using sealed records for proposed operations with a base abstract record carrying the OrderLineIdentifier, a ProposedOrderEvent record for EventType, ActorRole, and ActorPersonIdentifier, and an OrderMutationContext to track whether the actor is the order owner or a staff member.

The result type is OrderMutationValidationResult with an IsValid property that checks if the Errors collection is empty, and each OrderMutationError captures an optional OperationIndex (null for event-level errors), an error code enum, and a message. Now I'm writing the validator method that takes the proposed event, projection, and context to comprehensively check all invariants.

I'll start with the ledger lookup from the projection and track newly added lines in a HashSet. The validation flow checks that operations aren't empty, then enforces the post-close gate where closed sittings only allow staff operations (staff_edit, price_adjustment, fulfillment, fulfillment_reversal) by administrators, rejecting guest submissions entirely in that state.

Next I'm validating the actor's role against the event type using a capability matrix, then ensuring each operation matches the permitted kind for that event type. After that comes per-operation validation starting with LineAdd operations.

For LineAdd, I need to check that quantity falls within 1–100, verify the menu item exists and is active, and trust that the server provides the unit price. I'm also tracking which line IDs get added in this event and need to guard against duplicate line identifiers—either within the same event or colliding with existing ledger lines. The projection already ignores re-adds of existing IDs, but I should validate that fresh adds use new line IDs.

For LineRemoval, I'm enforcing several constraints: the target can't be a line added earlier in this same event, must already exist in the ledger, and can't be removed twice. For guest submissions specifically, they can only remove their own lines that are still pending.

For LinePriceAdjusted, the line must exist in the ledger, can't be removed, and requires a non-empty reason with a non-negative price. For LineFulfilled, the line must exist and be pending (not already fulfilled or removed). LineFulfillmentReverted follows similar patterns.

At the event level for guest submissions, I'm checking that the actor is the order owner and a sitting member, with the sitting still open—emitting SittingClosed if needed to avoid duplication with the post-close gate. For guest submissions, the actor needs to be a member, and on an open sitting the owner should qualify as one since the context provides both flags. I'm collecting all per-operation errors rather than short-circuiting so the client sees the full picture, even if role or type mapping creates noise in the per-op checks.

Guest submissions can mix adds and removals in the same batch—like sending a batch that adds new lines while removing their own pending ones—and invariant 5 prevents removing a line added in the same event anyway. Now I'm writing out the validator file with a clean structure: file-scoped namespace, XML docs, sealed classes, and a local helper function to collect errors by operation index.

Show less
Done
I have what I need for the validator. Writing it now — it's the last Domain piece and is integration-tested against every invariant, so I'll keep it faithful to §6.5 and the §6.4 capability matrix.

Architected DataAccess layer with DbUp migrations and connection management
Architected DataAccess layer with DbUp migrations and connection management

Inspect the migration SQL header

Find DbUp/migration specifics

Read ADR-0012 in full
Now I'm setting up the DataAccess project with references to Domain, Dapper, Npgsql, and DbUp, embedding the SQL migration files as resources. I'm creating a migration runner that uses DbUp to connect to Postgres with retry logic while the database boots up.

Building the upgrader with embedded scripts, transaction-per-script mode, and a PostgreSQL journal table to track applied migrations. If deployment fails, I'll log the error and crash the process immediately so the application doesn't start in an inconsistent state. I'll also expose a method to check if there are pending migrations for the readiness probe by calling IsUpgradeRequired() on the upgrader.

For the retry-while-booting logic, I need to handle the fact that the target database might not exist yet when EnsureDatabase runs, so I'll wrap the entire migration process in a retry loop that probes connectivity and handles the "database does not exist" error gracefully. Writing the retry wrapper... .LogToConsole() is available in DbUp, so I'll use that to keep things simple and avoid the interface implementation risk. I'll supplement it with high-level logging through ILogger from the caller side—things like "starting migrations," "applied N scripts," "up to date"—so we get structured logging in the OTel pipeline while DbUp's console output goes to stdout and gets captured by the container. ` is stable across versions. For the persistence layer, I need to define an interface with methods to create and open NpgsqlConnections, then implement it using NpgsqlDataSource as the underlying mechanism.

I'm configuring the csproj to embed SQL migration files...

Now I need to check the environment variable naming convention for the database connection string.

Architecting SchemaMigrationRunner with DbUp PostgreSQL integration
Architecting SchemaMigrationRunner with DbUp PostgreSQL integration

Cataloging WebApplication specs and health endpoint requirements
Cataloging WebApplication specs and health endpoint requirements

Create MyRestaurant.DataAccess.csproj — references Domain, Dapper/Npgsql/DbUp, embeds migration SQL

Create the connection factory abstraction and Npgsql implementation over an NpgsqlDataSource
I'm setting up the SchemaMigrationRunner using DbUp, configuring it to log to console for visibility and handle database creation with retry logic during startup. I need to use the DbUp API correctly—specifically the EnsureDatabase.For.PostgresqlDatabase() method from the dbup-postgresql package to ensure the database exists before running migrations.

Writing the runner class... I'll keep DataAccess lightweight by having the migration runner accept optional log delegates instead of depending on ILogger directly. DbUp will handle its own console output for detailed logs, while the runner exposes methods that take Action<string> callbacks for high-level status messages — Program can wire those directly to the logger. This avoids adding an unvalidated package dependency while still providing structured logging at the application layer.

For the retry logic, I need to handle transient exceptions like NpgsqlException, SocketException, and TimeoutException that occur when the database isn't ready yet. I'm defining the migration runner method with parameters for the connection string, optional log callbacks, maximum retry attempts, and a configurable retry delay that defaults to 2 seconds.

Writing the retry loop... On transient failures before the max attempts, I'll log a retry message and wait using a cancellation-aware delay mechanism before trying again. The final transient failure will rethrow, while a SchemaMigrationException from script execution fails fast without retry since it's a permanent error rather than a connectivity issue.

For the main program, I'm catching any exception from the upgrade run, logging it, and exiting with a non-zero code. I'm also adding a readiness check method that tests whether pending migrations exist by building the engine and checking if an upgrade is required—this connects to the database and returns false if anything fails. The journal table will be configured for PostgreSQL in the public schema, and I'm filtering embedded migration scripts from the assembly.

Now I need to write out the implementation file, making sure to include the necessary DbUp namespace imports since the static methods and extensions all live there.

In Program.cs, I'm loading environment variables into RestaurantOptions, validating them upfront to catch configuration issues early, and registering the database connection factory and other services.

For observability, I'm wiring up OpenTelemetry to collect traces from AspNetCore and Npgsql, metrics from the runtime and a custom meter, and sending everything to OTLP. I'm also configuring Blazor Server for interactive components. The migrations run after the app is built but before HTTP binding starts, so if they fail the startup exits with an error code. Health check endpoints at /healthz/live and /healthz/ready provide liveness and readiness signals, with the readiness check verifying the database is reachable and migrations are current.

Now I'm setting up the configuration layer with a RestaurantOptions POCO that reads from environment variables and validates them, a RestaurantMetrics class that defines the OpenTelemetry meter and instruments, an in-process domain event broadcaster for live updates, and the Blazor component structure. For the container setup, I'll have the app read ASPNETCORE_URLS from the environment with a sensible default, so the compose service can set it to http://+:8080 behind Caddy while local development uses launchSettings to bind to https://localhost:8443. Now I'm setting up Program.cs with the core application structure, starting with configuration validation using a bootstrap logger to fail fast if there are any configuration errors.

Writing OpenTelemetry setup... I need to add the necessary using statements for OpenTelemetry, then ensure IMeterFactory is registered in the dependency injection container by calling AddMetrics(), and finally register RestaurantMetrics as a service.

For the database connection factory, I'm registering the concrete NpgsqlDatabaseConnectionFactory as a singleton so the container can properly dispose of it, then forwarding the IDatabaseConnectionFactory interface to that implementation.

Registering the remaining singletons... Writing migration runner... The readiness check is a bit heavy since it opens a DB connection and reads the migration journal on every probe, but that's acceptable for a small app with modest compose healthcheck intervals. I could use a lighter approach like just SELECT 1, but the spec requires checking that migrations are current, so this is the right call. Now I'm setting up the Blazor Server interactivity with antiforgery middleware and mapping the root component before running the app. I need to be careful with UseStaticFiles since the Blazor framework files are served automatically by MapRazorComponents through the server middleware, not from wwwroot. I'll include it anyway since it's harmless and handles any actual static content. For antiforgery, UseAntiforgery is required after routing. On HTTPS redirection, since the app runs in a container behind Caddy with TLS termination upstream, I should skip UseHttpsRedirection to avoid redirect loops unless forwarded headers are properly configured.

For forwarded headers, I need to handle XForwardedProto and XForwardedFor so the app understands it's behind HTTPS and can properly detect the client origin—this matters for secure cookies and passkey authentication even in a skeleton. However, trusting all proxies is a security risk, so I'll add minimal forwarded headers configuration with appropriate constraints rather than leaving it unconfigured.

Now let me check the type names in Clock.cs and IdentifierFactory.cs.

Architecting multi-stage Containerfile with .NET runtime dependencies
Architecting multi-stage Containerfile with .NET runtime dependencies

Architected multi-stage containerization with compose profiles and deployment scripts
Architected multi-stage containerization with compose profiles and deployment scripts

Read §2 repository layout tree
I'm setting up a multi-stage Containerfile that builds the .NET application with the SDK, publishes it, then runs it on the ASP.NET runtime, making sure to include timezone data since the app needs to look up time zones for the restaurant configuration. The container will expose port 8080 and use dotnet as the entry point.

Now for the compose file, I'm wrestling with the profile strategy. The spec wants dev mode (default podman-compose up) to run web, postgres, and caddy, while production mode runs web, postgres, and cloudflared instead. The tricky part is that services without a profile key always start, so if I put caddy in a "dev" profile, it won't run on a plain up command. But if caddy has no profile, it'll also start in production mode when I use --profile production, which violates the spec. I need to figure out how to make the default behavior include caddy but exclude it when production is explicitly requested. LS configuration. Bare podman-compose up becomes a headless mode for quick testing with just web and postgres, exposing port 8080 to the host.

I'm second-guessing whether this aligns with the spec's intent—the docs say production runs without Caddy, and the profiles approach (dev profile for Caddy) handles that correctly. The "bare up = dev" phrasing in the spec is shorthand; run.sh should be the canonical dev entry point. I'll document this distinction clearly and make sure the setup is unambiguous.

Now mapping out the service configurations: Postgres uses environment variables from .env for credentials and a named volume for persistence, with a health check. The web service builds from the Containerfile, pulls in database connection details and other config from environment variables, depends on a healthy Postgres instance, includes a health check endpoint, and mounts a volume for data protection keys. It listens on port 8080 internally, and in dev mode Caddy proxies to it and exposes 8443 to the host.

For the dev environment, I'm setting up Caddy as a reverse proxy using the Alpine image with a mounted Caddyfile. In production, Cloudflared handles tunneling with a token from environment variables. The database connection string defaults to using the internal compose network hostname for Postgres.

For the web service healthcheck, I need curl since the ASP.NET image doesn't include it by default—I'll install it alongside tzdata in the runtime stage, then use a simple HTTP check against the /healthz/ready endpoint. For Postgres, I'm using the pg_isready command to verify the database is accepting connections.

Now I'm setting up the Caddyfile for local development with Caddy's internal CA for HTTPS on localhost, proxying requests to the web service. Then I'm writing a bash setup script that handles environment configuration, translates any Uptrace DSN settings to OpenTelemetry variables, detects the available container engine, and brings up the dev profile—deciding whether to run the web service in a container or locally with dotnet watch.

Actually, there's a networking issue here: if caddy runs in the compose network and proxies to web:8080, but the web app runs on the host via dotnet watch, caddy can't reach it. So the better approach is to skip caddy in dev-watch mode and let Kestrel serve HTTPS directly using the dev certificate from launchSettings, giving us a secure context without the extra container. That way compose only needs postgres, keeping things simpler.

The run.sh script would start postgres in a container with its port published to the host, set the database connection string to point at localhost:5432, and then run dotnet watch on the web project. For a full containerized stack, a --containers-only flag would spin up the entire compose setup including caddy and web, bypassing the local dotnet watch entirely.

Writing shell script... For the UPTRACE_DSN, I'll implement a best-effort translation: if it's set and OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT isn't already configured, I'll extract the endpoint and pass the full DSN as an uptrace-dsn header, which is what Uptrace actually expects. The backup script will use podman exec to run pg_dump inside the postgres container, saving to a timestamped file in the backup directory and pruning old backups after a successful dump. I'm also backing up the data protection keys volume by tarring it alongside the database—I'll mount the keys volume read-only and create a compressed archive in the backup directory. Then I'll move on to the restore script. Writing the Containerfile and scripts, making sure to use $COMPOSE exec for database operations so they work reliably across podman and docker regardless of container naming, and I'll validate the bash and YAML syntax before finishing. Writing the Containerfile... _HTTP_PORTS=8080 configures Kestrel to listen on port 8080. Now I'm moving on to the compose.yaml file. The key challenge is handling the DataProtection keys directory—since named volumes are root-owned by default, the non-root app user won't have write permissions. I need to either create and chown the directory in the Dockerfile before switching to the non-root user, or handle permissions through the volume mount configuration.

The complication with rootless podman is that named volumes are created as root:root inside the container, even though the container's root maps to the user's namespace. For the M1 scope, since DataProtection isn't actually used until M2, I'll document this as a known issue to verify later—the volume's writability for the non-root app user under rootless podman may need additional configuration or a workaround.

Actually, podman supports the :U volume mount option to automatically chown the volume to the container user, which solves this cleanly. I'll use that in the compose file with the short syntax. Docker would error on the podman-specific :U option, but since podman is the canonical target here, that's acceptable—I'll just note it.

For the compose setup, I should leverage depends_on with condition: service_healthy for proper service ordering, and configure the web service to read from .env using env_file while setting up the database connection string with variable interpolation and sensible defaults. Now I'm ready to write out the Containerfile.

Show less
Done

Architecting test project structure and file paths

Domain.Tests project file (xunit + NSubstitute over the pure Domain)
Setting up the DataAccess.Tests project with Testcontainers for PostgreSQL integration tests—these will be skipped in environments without Docker, but I'll include a real test with appropriate guards. DataAccess.Tests project file (Testcontainers-backed integration tests)

WebApplication.Tests project file (WebApplicationFactory host tests)

EndToEnd.Tests project file (Playwright browser E2E; populated in M6)
I'm starting to write the Domain unit tests, beginning with JoinTokenServiceTests.cs. I need to set up fixed test vectors that verify the token creation logic—given a specific secret, table GUID, and rotation window, the service should produce a deterministic token. The tests should validate that current and previous window tokens are accepted, future tokens are rejected, expired tokens are properly labeled, malformed input returns Invalid, and the NextWindowBoundary calculation is correct. The challenge is computing the expected token vector without being able to run .NET locally to calculate the HMAC.

I can use Python to compute the exact Base64Url-encoded token for a fixed test vector, which gives the test a real golden value to validate against. The message format is the lowercase-hyphenated GUID concatenated with a colon and the decimal window index, then HMAC-SHA256 hashed with the secret and Base64Url-encoded without padding. I'm picking a concrete test case: a 32-byte secret (bytes 0x00 through 0x1F), a table GUID like "0193c8f2-4b6a-7c3d-8e9f-a0b1c2d3e4f5", and a fixed Unix timestamp to calculate the window index.

Computing the golden tokens... Computing test data in Python...

Show less
Done
Now the real Domain unit tests. I'll compute genuine golden vectors for the join-token and PHC tests using Python (the sandbox has no .NET, but the crypto is standard), so the tests assert against real expected values rather than tautologies.


Architected test implementation strategy with token validation logic
Architected test implementation strategy with token validation logic

Compute golden HMAC join-token and PHC vectors for the Domain tests
I've got the Base64 URL encoding working correctly with the URL-safe alphabet and no padding, which aligns with Python's implementation. For the token creation, I'm passing a fixed Unix timestamp to the C# method so I can test the token generation consistently.

Now I'm setting up the test file with xunit, using the golden token values I've computed. The key test cases cover the window logic: TOKEN[W] is valid in the current window, TOKEN[W-1] is valid in the previous window, TOKEN[W-2] through TOKEN[W-10] are expired (within the lookback range), and TOKEN[W-11] is invalid (outside lookback). I'm also parsing the GUID in the expected format to match the token generation.

Golden vectors computed (C# Base64Url matches Python's URL-safe base64, no padding). Writing the Domain tests now.

Orchestrated comprehensive test suite implementation strategy
Orchestrated comprehensive test suite implementation strategy
Now I'm moving into Stage 1 with the remaining Domain tests. I have the APIs and golden vectors ready, so I'll write out the test classes efficiently: Argon2PhcStringTests covering the PHC vector with format round-tripping and rehash detection, ObligationsPipelineTests for the state machine that enforces password before TOTP, and OrderProjectionTests.

Then I'll tackle the validator and generator tests—OrderMutationValidatorTests hitting every rule from §6.5 with both pass and fail cases, and SecretGeneratorTests verifying the PairingCode alphabet and length plus RecoveryCode batch generation.

After that come smoke tests for the other three projects using Testcontainers for the database, RestaurantOptions binding validation, and a placeholder for end-to-end tests.

Finally I'll wrap up with scripts, environment files, documentation, and validation checks before presenting the full file structure.

Let me start with the Argon2PhcStringTests using the computed PHC vector where the salt is bytes 0–15 and the tag is bytes 100–131, then verify that parsing and formatting round-trip correctly.

RequiresRehash compares stored and configured parameters for equality, so matching parameters return false while different ones return true. For malformed inputs, TryParse rejects empty strings, non-PHC prefixes, wrong algorithms like argon2i, incorrect versions, missing cost fields, and invalid salt lengths.

I'm implementing a state machine for the obligations pipeline that determines which action to take based on password and TOTP enrollment requirements. The logic maps four possible states to their corresponding obligations—prioritizing password changes when both are needed—and only considers the requirements satisfied when neither action is required.

Unit tests for the pure order-event fold / projection (§8.5)
Now I'm tackling the OrderMutationValidatorTests.cs file, which needs comprehensive coverage of every rule from section 6.5 with both success and failure scenarios. The validator takes a proposed order event, the current order projection, and a mutation context, so I need to construct test fixtures for the proposed event with its event type, actor role, actor identifier, and operations, along with the projection state and context needed to validate against all the mutation rules.

For the operations themselves, I'm building test data for line additions with menu item validation, line removals with optional reasons, price adjustments with justification, fulfillments, and fulfillment reversals. Rather than hand-constructing the OrderProjectionResult with its ledger entries, I'll seed it using OrderProjection.FromEvents on some initial events — that gives me a realistic ledger with proper tracking of who added each line, whether items are removed or fulfilled, and it also validates the projection logic itself in the process.

Now I'm mapping out the error codes and designing a test case for each one. At the event level, I need to cover scenarios like proposing an event with no operations, attempting post-close edits by non-administrators, and guest submissions after the sitting closes. Some scenarios will trigger multiple error codes at once — for instance, a guest submission after close will fail both because the guest role isn't administrator and because guest submissions are forbidden post-close, so I'll need to assert that the specific codes are present.

For operation-level errors, I'm looking at role restrictions like Kitchen staff attempting price adjustments or guests trying staff edits, invalid operation types within certain events, and guest submission constraints like the actor needing to be the order owner and a sitting member. I also need to test quantity boundaries and menu item validation.

Continuing with removal operations, I'm checking that the line being removed actually exists in the order and hasn't already been removed in the current projection.

For staff edits, I need to catch cases where they add and remove the same line within a single event. For guest removals, there are stricter rules — guests can only remove lines they themselves added, and only if those lines haven't been fulfilled yet. Since guests can only create guest_submission events, and those events permit add/remove operations, a guest removing their own pending line works through a guest_submission with a ProposedLineRemoval.

When testing these removal scenarios, I'll set ActorIsOrderOwner and ActorIsSittingMember to true so the validation focuses on the removal-specific errors. The key distinction is whether the line exists in the ledger, who added it, and whether it's still pending or already fulfilled.

For price adjustments, I need to cover LineRemoved (targeting a removed line), ReasonMissing (empty or whitespace reason), and the case where the line itself doesn't exist in the order. Fulfillment tests should check that the target line is actually pending, and reversal tests should verify the line was fulfilled before attempting to reverse it.

Then I'm laying out the happy path scenarios: guest submissions adding lines, staff edits like kitchen adding items, price adjustments with valid reasons, fulfillments on pending lines, and reversals of fulfilled lines. There's also the special case where an administrator can make corrections even after the sitting closes. Finally, I'm testing that when multiple operations fail, all errors get collected rather than stopping at the first one.

Now I need to build the test infrastructure. I'll create a projection builder method that takes a sequence of events and reconstructs the order state, along with factories for generating consistent test events and operations. I'm defining a set of fixed GUIDs for the key entities—the order itself, various guest and staff actors, different line items in various states, and some inventory items—so I can reference them consistently throughout the tests.

For the seed data, I'm setting up an order with multiple line items that demonstrate different lifecycle states: one line that stays pending, one that gets fulfilled, one that gets removed, and another added by staff. Then I'll trace through the event sequence to show how each operation transforms the ledger state.

Now I'm defining builder helpers for constructing proposed events—one for each event type like guest submissions, staff edits, price adjustments, fulfillments, and reversals. I'll also create factories for the proposed operations themselves to keep the test setup clean.

For context, I'm building reusable OrderMutationContext instances that represent different actor and sitting states, so I can vary them across test cases. The key thing I need to watch is the uniqueness validation in AddLine—if a line ID already exists in the ledger or gets added twice in the same event, it should fail with LineIdentifierNotUnique.

For the happy path where staff adds a valid line, I'll use a fresh NewLine guid that isn't in the ledger yet. The MenuItemExists and IsActive flags are provided as true for these cases. Now I'm thinking through the post-close admin correction scenario: an Administrator can issue a StaffEdit event after the sitting closes, and since RolePermittedForEventType allows this and the operation itself is valid within a staff edit, the add should go through.

But actually, adding a line to a closed sitting doesn't quite fit the "correction" model semantically. I'll switch to a PriceAdjustment event instead—it's more clearly corrective and realistic. An Administrator proposes a price adjustment targeting an existing line with a reason and non-negative price, which passes validation when the sitting is closed.

For the happy path with an open sitting and a Counter actor: targeting an existing pending line with a reason and valid price works fine.

Now checking if a guest can remove their own pending line: a GuestSubmission event with a remove operation on a line they added themselves passes validation—it's their own guest line, not fulfilled, so no errors. That's a valid happy path to include.

But if a guest tries to remove a line added by staff, that fails the ownership check.

And if a guest tries to remove their own line but it's already fulfilled, the validation catches that it's not pending anymore.

Now checking the removal target scenarios: when a staff member adds and removes a line in the same edit, the system tracks that it was added in this event so it catches the RemovalTargetsLineAddedInSameEvent error. If someone tries to remove a line that doesn't exist, that's LineNotInOrder. If the line's already been removed, LineAlreadyRemoved catches it.

For price adjustments, if the target line was already removed, that's PriceAdjustmentLineRemoved. Missing a reason string triggers PriceAdjustmentReasonMissing. Negative prices get caught whether they come from a price adjustment or a new line addition—both route to UnitPriceNegative. Now looking at fulfillment line validation...

When Kitchen tries to fulfill a line that's already fulfilled or removed, that's FulfillmentLineNotPending. For reversals, if the line isn't actually fulfilled yet, we get FulfillmentReversalLineNotFulfilled. The role check is interesting—if Kitchen attempts a price adjustment, they don't have permission for that event type, so I need to construct a valid price adjustment operation on an existing line with a reason, then verify the error is ActorRoleNotPermittedForEventType at the event level. I could also test with a Guest proposing a staff edit instead.

Now checking the operation-level constraints: if a StaffEdit contains a fulfillment operation, that's not allowed for that event type, so it should error with OperationNotAllowedForEventType and skip further validation of that operation. An event with no operations at all should trigger EventHasNoOperations. And for quantities, adding a new line with zero quantity should fail with QuantityOutOfRange.

For menu items, if the item doesn't exist in the menu, that's MenuItemNotFound. If it exists but is inactive, that's MenuItemInactive. If trying to add a line with an identifier that's already in the ledger, that's LineIdentifierNotUnique. And when a guest tries to submit an order but isn't the owner, that should fail with GuestSubmissionActorNotOrderOwner.

Now checking the actor permissions more carefully: if the actor is the owner but not a sitting member, that's GuestSubmissionActorNotSittingMember. For staff edits after the sitting closes, only administrators can make changes—otherwise PostCloseActorNotAdministrator. Guest submissions are completely forbidden once the sitting closes, which triggers PostCloseGuestSubmissionForbidden, and this should also include the PostCloseActorNotAdministrator error. Finally, when a staff edit contains multiple validation failures across different lines, all errors should be returned together rather than stopping at the first one.

Now looking at the valid scenarios: a guest submission succeeds when the owner adds a new line item that exists and is active with valid quantity and price, with the sitting open and proper context. Staff edits by kitchen staff are valid regardless of sitting state or member status. Price adjustments work when the counter adjusts an existing line with a new price and reason while the sitting is open. Fulfillment succeeds when kitchen marks a pending line as fulfilled. And reversals work when kitchen reverts a fulfilled line back to pending.

For post-close scenarios, an administrator can adjust prices even after the sitting closes. Staff and kitchen events don't check actor ownership or membership—only guest submissions do—so I can set those flags to false, but the sitting must be open for the happy paths.

I'm creating a helper function to build contexts with configurable open, owner, and member flags, and another to assert that specific error codes appear in validation results.

Now I need to construct the seed projection as a static field using OrderProjection.FromEvents, which requires building OrderEvent objects through the factory. I'll define distinct GUIDs for the actors and ledger entries, making sure to separate new lines from unknown lines and seeded lines, then wire up the guest submission proposal so the actor person identifier matches the ledger entry's owner for the "own" check.

Writing test file...

Show less
Done
Now the most important test file — the §6.5 mutation validator, covering every error code plus happy paths.


Drafting test cases for PairingCode generation
Drafting test cases for PairingCode generation

Exhaustive unit tests for the §6.5 order mutation validator (every rule, both outcomes)
I just realized I used a Cyrillic "П" character instead of a regular Latin "P" in a parameter name across the test file — specifically in the PropAdjust helper and the test method call. While C# technically allows Unicode letters in identifiers so it would compile fine, this is definitely a mistake I need to fix.

Let me replace all instances of newПrice with the proper ASCII newPrice to clean this up. I'll target the named argument call in the admin test and the helper method signature and body where it's used.

Done
I introduced a non-ASCII character (Cyrillic "П") in a parameter name by mistake. It would technically compile since it's consistent, but it's a latent trap. Let me fix all occurrences to plain newPrice.

Locate the stray non-ASCII identifier occurrences

Replace Cyrillic П with ASCII and verify the file is now pure ASCII

Now I'm setting up tests for the SecretGenerator and PairingCode — I need to verify that Generate() produces an 8-character code using only the valid alphabet, confirm the alphabet excludes look-alike characters like I, L, O, 0, and 1, and test the recovery functionality.

For the batch generation, I'm checking that GenerateBatch() returns exactly 10 distinct codes, all non-empty. I haven't seen the full format of GenerateOne yet, so I'll focus on the count and uniqueness rather than asserting specific formatting details like separators.

Examined migration runner signatures and test framework requirements
Examined migration runner signatures and test framework requirements

See RecoveryCode.GenerateOne format for accurate assertions
Now I'm setting up the test assertions for the SecretGenerator—checking that PairingCode has a length of 8 characters, that the generated codes match that length, and verifying the alphabet excludes easily confused characters like I, L, O, 0, and 1.

The alphabet contains 31 distinct characters. I need to test RecoveryCode.GenerateBatch() to ensure it produces 10 distinct codes in the XXXXX-XXXXX format, and verify that SecretGenerator creates join secrets of 32 bytes, TOTP secrets of 20 bytes, and device secrets that decode properly from base64url. I'll run the pairing code generator multiple times to validate the character set.

Read RestaurantOptions to write accurate binding/validation tests
I'm checking that Validate() returns an empty list for valid configs rather than throwing exceptions, and I need to use FromConfiguration() to load settings. I'll construct the configuration through ConfigurationBuilder with in-memory data, which requires Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration — this should be available in the test project since it references the web app and those packages are part of the shared framework. AddInMemoryCollection is part of Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration, which lives in the ASP.NET Core shared framework, so adding the explicit FrameworkReference to Microsoft.AspNetCore.App will ensure it's available. I'll update the test project's csproj to include that reference, then write a test that validates an empty configuration — which should produce exactly one error for the missing database connection string while the other settings fall back to their defaults.

Then I'm testing the binding behavior: with an empty config, RestaurantName defaults to "My Restaurant", PublicOrigin to https://localhost:8443, and RelyingPartyId to "localhost". When I set environment variables like RESTAURANT_PUBLIC_ORIGIN and RESTAURANT_NAME, those override the defaults and bind correctly. I'm also checking the floor guards — setting Argon2 memory too low or token rotation too short should trigger validation errors.

Architected three backup and tunnel scripts with container engine detection
Architected three backup and tunnel scripts with container engine detection

Read run.sh to mirror compose detection and env handling in the new scripts
Now I'm writing the three backup and restore scripts, each with built-in compose-engine detection to keep them standalone. I'll load the .env file to pull in the database credentials and backup configuration variables, then start with the backup script that runs pg_dump inside the postgres container.

For the backup script specifically, I need to handle the pg_dump output carefully—dumping to a temporary file first so that if the command fails, I don't leave a partial or corrupted dump behind. I also need to back up the dataprotection keys volume using the container engine's run command with a volume mount, and then prune old backups down to the retention count once everything succeeds. The tricky part is detecting whether we're using podman or docker so I can call the right engine for the volume backup. For the keys backup, I'll use a busybox container to tar up the dataprotection volume and write it to the backup directory with a timestamped filename, keeping in mind that rootless podman on Fedora needs the :z SELinux label when mounting host paths.

Actually, streaming the tar output directly to the host avoids mounting the host directory entirely, which sidesteps SELinux complications and file ownership issues. I could use busybox for this since it's lightweight, though the postgres image already has tar built in—but that feels wasteful just for archiving. so the dump can run independently of whether the web service is running. For the restore script, I'll stop the web container, pipe the dump file into pg_restore with the --clean and --if-exists flags to handle existing data, then restart web. I could optionally restore the DP keys from a tar file too, but I'll keep the restore focused on the database and mention key restoration in comments for now.

For the quick tunnel script, I need to add a loud warning about the PSL passkey since it's a security consideration, set up a state directory under .tunnels/<name>/ to track the PID, URL, and logs (git-ignored), then start cloudflared with a quick tunnel pointing to localhost:8080 and capture the generated HTTPS URL.

Since cloudflared might not be installed on the host, I'll run it through the container engine instead—using docker run or podman run with --network host so it can reach the local web server, then poll the logs to extract the tunnel URL.

I'll structure this as a subcommand script with start, stop, and status actions, defaulting to starting a demo tunnel. For the container, I'll use --name to make it easy to stop later with a force remove, and I'll capture the process ID when backgrounding so the stop action can clean it up. I should also add .tunnels/ to .gitignore and validate the bash syntax before finishing.

Now I'm writing the backup script.

Show less
Done
Now the three operational scripts, mirroring run.sh's compose detection. Starting with backup.sh.


Orchestrating setup tasks and validating configurations
Orchestrating setup tasks and validating configurations

Restore script: stop web, pg_restore --clean into postgres, restart web; optional keys restore

Drafting README.md with essential documentation sections
Drafting README.md with essential documentation sections

Ignore quick-tunnel state directory
.gitignore
+6
-3

Mark scripts executable and syntax-check them all
The chmod command executed successfully but the files still show as non-executable in the listing, which suggests the output mount might not preserve executable bits or is showing a cached view of the filesystem. Since these are being delivered as regular files rather than through git, the exec bit may not persist in the way it would through version control.

I'll document in the README that users should run chmod +x run.sh scripts/*.sh as part of the quick start, since they can either execute with ./run.sh (which requires the bit set) or use bash run.sh directly. Let me try the chmod one more time and check the result with stat to see if it actually takes this time.


Retry setting exec bit and stat to confirm

Finalizing deliverable structure and presenting files

The [[ errors came from running under sh instead of bash, which broke my conditional logic and falsely reported missing Domain files — GlobalUsings.cs is actually there. I need to re-run the verification with bash and simpler test syntax. The Cyrillic scan came back clean with no detected codepoints, but there seems to be something else to investigate there.

The grep command for detecting Cyrillic characters failed because the regex syntax wasn't accepted properly — the error about character code point values being too large suggests the shell mangled the escape sequences, so the "clean" result is unreliable. I should switch to Python for the Cyrillic scan to avoid shell escaping issues, and explicitly invoke bash for the xunit loop check instead of relying on the tool's default shell.

Actually, the earlier per-file grep worked fine and found the 6 missing files. The real issue is just my bracket syntax. Orchestrated summary, staged plan, and critical caveats

Final listing of all Stage 1 files

Presented 3 files
Now I'm putting together a concise summary with the staged plan the user requested, along with the key caveats to keep in mind.

Done
Stage 1 is complete — 59 files, the full skeleton plus the entire pure-Domain layer and its test suite. BUILD_PROGRESS.md is the "start here" document.

The staged plan
Six stages aligned to the spec's milestones (§19), each meant to leave the tree buildable and testable:

Stage 1 — M1: skeleton + pure Domain ✅ (this deliverable)
Stage 2 — M2: identity & accounts — Dapper Identity stores, Argon2id hasher (floor guard + concurrency semaphore), passkeys, TOTP + recovery codes, lockout, the obligations middleware, /setup first-admin bootstrap, roles/policies, security-event logging.
Stage 3 — M3: tables & joining — table CRUD + secret rotation, display pairing + device cookie, /display, the rotating-token endpoints + metrics, grant cookie, sittings + membership.
Stage 4 — M4: ordering — the living order with FOR SHARE/FOR UPDATE locking, staging UI, batch send + §6.5 validation, staff edits, fulfillment/reversal, view≡fold tests, kitchen surface + reminder background service.
Stage 5 — M5: counter & administration — bills, price adjustment, close & settle, end-of-day, counter fallback QR, menu management + events, event explorer, hide/unhide, post-close corrections.
Stage 6 — M6: hardening — the 15 Playwright E2E scenarios (§16.3), backup/restore drill, cloudflared production wiring, CI.
Each stage is self-contained so a session limit between them doesn't strand you mid-feature.

What Stage 1 contains
The Domain layer is complete and BCL-only: the order-event fold (OrderProjection), the full §6.5 mutation validator (OrderMutationValidator), the rotating HMAC join-token service, the Argon2 PHC string helper, the obligations state machine, UUIDv7 identifiers, and the secret/pairing/recovery generators. DataAccess has the verbatim §8.2/§8.3 schema (22 tables, 5 views) and the fail-fast DbUp runner. WebApplication has the Blazor host wired in the right order — validate config → telemetry → migrate before binding HTTP → health endpoints. Plus the full container/compose/Caddy/tunnel infrastructure and the backup/restore/quick_tunnel scripts.

Tests are real, not stubs: the Domain suite uses golden crypto vectors I computed independently, and OrderMutationValidatorTests exercises every validation rule in both directions. The DataAccess test runs the actual migrations against PostgreSQL 17 via Testcontainers.

Two things to know before you build
The environment I generated this in had no .NET SDK and couldn't reach NuGet, so none of the C# has been compiled and the package versions in Directory.Packages.props are unverified. On your Fedora box:

bash
chmod +x run.sh scripts/*.sh   # exec bit doesn't survive transfer
dotnet restore                  # adjust any package version that doesn't resolve
dotnet build                    # fix anything the compiler catches
dotnet test                     # Domain + WebApplication need no services; DataAccess needs Podman
./run.sh                        # confirm migrations apply and /healthz/ready → 200
BUILD_PROGRESS.md records the deliberate decisions and the likely rough edges — the DbUp LogToConsole call is the single most probable spot for a version-driven build break, so check there first if dotnet build complains in DataAccess.

Want me to go straight into Stage 2, or would you rather compile Stage 1 first and tell me what the build surfaces?
